visual_checks: Identify all form fields that collect personal user information (name, email, phone, address, credit card, etc.)
Check the HTML for autocomplete attributes on these input fields
Verify the autocomplete values match the expected input purpose from the HTML specification
Look for login forms with username/email and password fields — do they have autocomplete='username' and autocomplete='current-password'?
Check address forms for appropriate autocomplete values (street-address, postal-code, country, etc.)
pass_conditions: Input fields that collect standard personal information have appropriate autocomplete attribute values
The autocomplete values match the list of input purposes defined in WCAG 2.1 Section 7 (e.g., 'name', 'email', 'tel', 'street-address')
Login forms use autocomplete='username' and autocomplete='current-password' or 'new-password'
Credit card fields use autocomplete='cc-number', 'cc-exp', 'cc-name', etc.
fail_conditions: Form fields collecting personal information (name, email, phone, address) lack autocomplete attributes
Autocomplete attribute values are incorrect for the field's purpose (e.g., autocomplete='off' on a name field)
Login form fields have no autocomplete attributes
autocomplete='off' is used on fields that should support autofill for user convenience
Fields have autocomplete values that do not match their actual purpose
na_conditions: Page contains no form fields that collect personal user information
Form fields collect information not in the WCAG Section 7 input purposes list (e.g., custom business data fields)
Page has no forms at all
ne_conditions: AI response could not be parsed
Form HTML could not be captured to inspect autocomplete attributes
Form fields are dynamically rendered and not present in the initial DOM
common_mistakes: Using autocomplete='off' to prevent browser autofill instead of proper autocomplete values
Missing autocomplete on common fields like email, telephone, and address
Using autocomplete='on' as a generic value instead of specific purpose tokens
Incorrect autocomplete values (e.g., autocomplete='name' on an email field)
Password fields using autocomplete='off' instead of 'current-password' or 'new-password'

examples.pass: A registration form has <input autocomplete='given-name'>, <input autocomplete='family-name'>, <input autocomplete='email'>, and <input autocomplete='new-password'>, allowing browsers and assistive technologies to autofill and personalize fields
examples.fail: A checkout form collects name, email, address, and credit card information but none of the input fields have autocomplete attributes, preventing browsers from identifying the fields' purposes
examples.partial: A contact form has autocomplete='email' on the email field but the name field and phone field lack autocomplete attributes

plain_meaning: Form fields that collect information about the user (name, email, phone, address, credit card, etc.) must identify their purpose programmatically using HTML autocomplete tokens (or equivalent) from the WCAG-listed Input Purposes. The intent is that browsers and assistive technologies can auto-fill or relabel fields for users who benefit from consistent identification -- including users with cognitive disabilities who rely on symbol-based or personalized presentations.
scope_applies_to: Form fields collecting information ABOUT THE USER (personal name, email, phone, postal address, date of birth, credit card details, username/password)
Any text input, select, or similar that maps to one of the 53 WCAG Input Purposes
scope_does_not_apply_to: Fields that do NOT collect user information (search boxes, query fields, content-entry fields, comment boxes)
Fields whose purpose is not on the WCAG Input Purposes list (e.g., 'favorite color', arbitrary survey fields)
Missing or broken visible labels -- that belongs to SC 3.3.2 / 1.3.1
Missing programmatic name on a control with no visible label -- that belongs to SC 4.1.2
Fields in a technology that does not support autocomplete tokens (the criterion has a technology-support caveat)
borderline_calibration: Only fields matching one of the 53 named WCAG Input Purposes need an autocomplete token. A quiz 'what is your favorite pet' field is out of scope.
autocomplete='off' on a user-info field that should be identifiable is a failure when no other mechanism conveys purpose.
Incorrect autocomplete value (e.g., autocomplete='email' on a phone field) is a hard failure.
Missing autocomplete on an obvious user-info field (email, name, phone, address) is a hard failure at medium+.
aria-describedby with type hints does not substitute for autocomplete; the rule specifically calls for the token mechanism.
